event_blocks.summary: On the exposed sandy beach of Kiribati's Tarawa atoll, under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds and gentle background music, a Man in a purple sarong, a Man in blue patterned shorts, a Man in gray t-shirt, and a Man in white tank top, along with a Young boy in a striped shirt, are engaged in the urgent task of collecting large coral pieces and seaweed at low tide to build a protective wall. One man explains that the scarcity of stones necessitates gathering coral from further out due to the sea flooding their house, a home shared by Tiatata's family of 12, who have been constructing this coral wall for 10 years. As they move the heavy coral, balancing it on their shoulders and adding it to a growing pile near the shore, another man describes the severity of high tides and strong winds, noting that their higher, more stable wall, unlike many others that collapse, still stands. The narrator highlights the vulnerability of the low-lying Kiribati coastline, emphasizing with an aerial view that Teata's wall may cease to offer protection within 30 years, as a documentary title and report information appear on screen against a backdrop of palm trees and poignant music.

Man in white tank top.description: Wearing a white tank top and dark shorts, walks with the group and assists in carrying coral.

Coral pile.description: A growing stack of large, rough coral rocks being built up by the men, located near the shoreline under a makeshift shelter, serving as a crucial protective barrier against rising waters, though its long-term effectiveness is questioned by a narrator.

Man in purple sarong.description: Shirtless, wearing a purple sarong, actively collecting and carrying large coral pieces, later seen placing them at the pile, contributing to the protective wall whose construction and purpose are discussed by a man working on it.

Aerial view of Kiribati coastline.description: A wide, sweeping shot showing the low-lying island, turquoise waters, and sparse vegetation, emphasizing the island's extreme vulnerability to rising sea levels, a threat highlighted by the on-screen text 'DocFilm KIRIBATI: A Drowning Paradise in the South Pacific' and a narrator's warning about the future of protective walls.

raw_segments: The video opens on a wide, exposed sandy beach under a bright blue sky with scattered clouds, revealing coral fragments and seaweed at low tide on Tarawa, the main atoll of the island nation of Kiribati. A gentle, rhythmic background music plays as a narrator explains that Tiatata and his three sons have only a few hours before the water returns. Four shirtless men, including one in a purple sarong and another in blue patterned shorts, move purposefully across the terrain, collecting large coral pieces. One man, likely Tiatata or one of his sons, explains in a translated voice, "There are no stones on the beach anymore, and that's why we have to go far out. We need to get coral because the sea is flooding our house." The men, some balancing heavy coral on their shoulders, carry the pieces towards a growing pile of coral rocks near the shore, where a young boy in a blue and yellow striped shirt observes. The narrator elaborates that the home of Tiatata and his family of 12 is under threat, and for 10 years they have been building this coral wall. Another man's translated voice adds, "When the water comes all the way up to here, and there's a strong wind..." The scene, with the DW logo visible in the top left, conveys communal labor against the tropical backdrop of palm trees, driven by the urgent need to protect their home from the encroaching sea.

The minute opens with a close-up of a shirtless, wet Man in purple sarong, urgently collecting large coral pieces from the shoreline and adding them to a growing Coral pile near a makeshift shelter. Other men, including the Man in blue patterned shorts and the Man in white tank top, assist in this deliberate effort to build the barrier, while a Young boy in striped shirt observes from under the shelter. The sounds of coral being moved accompany a man's voice explaining the necessity of their work: "That's when it's worst. The water bashes against our wall and sprays over our whole house. We have to build a higher wall so the water doesn't flood our home." He continues, describing how "In the past, our wall kept breaking because it was only this high. Now it's higher and more stable. It protects us from the sea," noting that while many walls collapse at high tide, theirs is still standing. The camera then pans across the sandy shoreline with scattered debris and palm trees, before transitioning to a wide aerial shot of the low-lying Kiribati coastline, showcasing turquoise waters and emphasizing the island's vulnerability. A narrator's voice somberly warns that "In just 30 years, Teata's wall could cease to offer any protection from the sea," as on-screen text appears, reading 'DocFilm KIRIBATI: A Drowning Paradise in the South Pacific' and 'A Report by Markus Henssler,' all set against a backdrop of bright, clear skies and underscored by a poignant, documentary-style background music.

event_blocks.summary: Aerial views of the Kiribati coastline reveal a narrow land strip flanked by turquoise waters, with a narrator explaining that this low-lying island state, averaging only 2 meters above sea level, faces an existential threat from climate change. Dramatic music and the sound of rushing water and crashing waves accompany scenes of coastal erosion and flooding, as rising sea levels, storm surges, and severe tropical storms inundate the country with increasing frequency. Turbulent waves crash over seawalls, flooding residential areas and submerging a thatched hut, while vehicles navigate a flooded road. A woman holding a child in waist-deep water illustrates the daily reality of survival, as the narrator states, "For the locals, it's a question of survival." The final aerial shot shows extensive flooding across the island, with homes and roads partially submerged, and a monument with a bird sculpture standing amidst the rising waters, symbolizing endurance against the looming threat.

A male narrator identifies it as 'the island state of Kiribati,' one of the lowest-lying countries, consisting of 33 atolls spread between Hawaii and Australia, with over 100,000 residents on Tarawa.

A male narrator attributes this to 'sea levels rising, storm surges and severe tropical storms' caused by climate change, while the distinct sound of rushing water and crashing waves is heard.

raw_segments: The segment opens with sweeping aerial views of the low-lying Kiribati coastline, its narrow land strip flanked by turquoise waters and sparse vegetation, as a male narrator introduces "the island state of Kiribati" as one of the lowest-lying countries on Earth. He explains that its "narrow, unprotected atolls in the South Pacific are on average only 2 meters above sea level," while a world map highlights Kiribati's remote location between Hawaii and Australia, zooming into Tarawa, where "more than half its 100,000 residents live." A gentle, slightly melancholic background music plays as the narrator poses the urgent question, "but for how much longer?" The serene beauty abruptly transitions to scenes of coastal erosion and flooding, accompanied by intensifying, dramatic music and the distinct sound of rushing water and crashing waves. The narrator explains that "climate change is threatening the people of Kiribati," with "sea levels rising, storm surges and severe tropical storms are flooding the sparsely populated country with increasing frequency." Turbulent waves crash over seawalls, inundating homes and roads, with water surging into residential areas and under a house’s porch. A thatched hut is shown nearly submerged by relentless waves, and vehicles navigate a flooded road as waves crash over the barrier, the sounds of water becoming very prominent. The narrative then shifts to a more intimate human perspective, showing a woman holding a child in waist-deep water, illustrating daily life amidst the encroaching sea, as the narrator states, "For the locals, it's a question of survival." The final aerial shot reveals extensive flooding across the island, with homes and roads partially submerged, and a prominent monument standing resiliently amidst the rising waters, symbolizing both endurance and the looming threat, all underscored by the somber music and persistent sounds of the sea.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view of the Kiribati coastline reveals a flooded residential area adjacent to a government building marked 'OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT', 'PUBLIC SERVICE OFFICE', and 'FOREIGN AFFAIRS UPSTAIRS', where the narrator states climate change is a national priority for Kiribati. Choi Yeeting, the climate commissioner since 2010, walks toward the building and is later seen driving a vehicle through a tropical environment. He then stands barefoot on a sandy shore in a patterned shirt, observing a flooded residential zone with homes partially submerged in calm turquoise water, surrounded by mangroves and palm trees, gesturing with concern. Choi Yeeting expresses worry about the community's living conditions, citing potential sanitation problems and contamination of drinkable water, noting that while it's not a king tide, he imagines the devastating impact of full submersion. An aerial drone shot emphasizes the vulnerability of the narrow, low-lying island settlement with scattered homes, vegetation, and a lagoon. The narrator concludes that the people of Kiribati may eventually have to leave their country, with the government having already purchased land.

raw_segments: The segment opens with a wide shot of a formal administrative setting, featuring a government building labeled 'OFFICE OF THE PRESIDENT', 'PUBLIC SERVICE OFFICE', and 'FOREIGN AFFAIRS UPSTAIRS'. A narrator immediately establishes the context, stating that climate change is a national priority for the government of Kiribati. The scene transitions to Choi Yeeting, identified by the narrator as the official climate commissioner since 2010 and alarmed by climate change, walking toward the building's entrance. He is then seen driving a vehicle through a lush, tropical environment. The narrative shifts to a somber coastal area where Choi Yeeting, now barefoot and wearing a black-and-white patterned shirt, stands on a sandy shore. He observes a flooded residential zone with homes partially submerged in calm turquoise water, surrounded by mangroves and palm trees, conveying concern as he gestures toward the water. Speaking with a calm but worried tone, he describes the 'worrying' conditions where the community has to live, highlighting potential sanitation problems and contamination of drinkable water. The camera pans across the flooded landscape, showing houses on stilts and debris along the shoreline, emphasizing the impact of rising sea levels. Choi Yeeting further explains that while it's not currently a king tide or a significant sea-level rise, he imagines the devastating impact when a full submersion by water occurs. An aerial drone shot reveals the broader context of a narrow, low-lying island with scattered homes, vegetation, and a lagoon, underscoring the settlement's extreme vulnerability to climate change. The overall atmosphere is somber and reflective, underscored by the narrator's concluding statement that one day the people of Kiribati will have to leave their country, and the government has already purchased land.

event_blocks.summary: Aerial views of Kiribati's low-lying coastline reveal scattered homes partially submerged, illustrating the impact of sea-level rise. On a sandy beach, a group of men, including one in a yellow shirt with 'Kiribati Independence 31st Anniversary 2018' on the back, a man in a gray t-shirt, and a man in a white t-shirt with a 'USP' logo, push the red and white motorized boat named 'SHAMROCK' into shallow, clear water. The man in the white t-shirt, identified as Tatau, holding a water bottle and an orange rope, explains to the camera that the area, once a beach, is now flooded, with rocks washed ashore, necessitating the daily relocation of their boat to prevent damage. Aerial shots then show flooded residential areas and a small island with a thatched hut. The narrator mentions a coral wall built by the family for protection, and Tatau elaborates on the rising water levels, predicting the eventual disappearance of the land but expressing their determination to remain, "like an island in the water," as the video concludes with another aerial perspective of the inundated island.

He speaks directly to the camera, explaining that the area is flooded, what used to be beach is now gone, and they have to move their boat daily to prevent it from being smashed. He further states that the water is continuously rising, and eventually, the land will disappear, but they will stay and 'float like an island in the water'.

raw_segments: The video opens with sweeping aerial views of a low-lying island in Kiribati, showcasing its narrow coastline, turquoise lagoon, and scattered homes partially submerged in water, emphasizing the vulnerability to sea-level rise. The scene transitions to a group of men on a sandy beach pushing a red and white motorized boat named 'SHAMROCK' into the shallow, clear water. A narrator explains that "Every day, Tatau and his three brothers have to carry their fishing boat into the sea. It's hard work." Among the men, one in a yellow shirt with 'Kiribati Independence 31st Anniversary 2018' printed on the back leads the effort, while others, including a man in a gray t-shirt and a man in a white t-shirt with a 'USP' logo, assist, wading into the water and guiding the boat with its yellow buoyancy floats. The man in the white t-shirt, identified by the narrator as Tatau, holds a water bottle and an orange rope, speaking directly to the camera and gesturing toward the flooded shoreline. He explains, "As you can see, this area is flooded. All this from here to all the way back there used to be beach. Now the beach is gone and the rocks are washed up. That's why we have to move our boat every day, otherwise it would get smashed." The video cuts back to aerial shots of flooded residential areas, with homes surrounded by water and vegetation, and a small island with a thatched hut and a few structures, highlighting the extent of coastal inundation. The narrator adds that "The family defies the rising sea levels with a coral wall that resembles a fortress." Tatau reappears, continuing his explanation while pointing to the flooded land, stating, "The water is rising and rising. Eventually, the land over there will disappear. But we'll stay and float like an island in the water," reinforcing the environmental challenges faced by the community. The sequence ends with another aerial view of the flooded island, underscoring the fragility of the coastal environment.

event_blocks.summary: An aerial view of Kiribati's coastline reveals a low-lying island threatened by encroaching turquoise waters, a stark symbol of the nation's fight for survival. Inside a thatched hut, an older woman, Mangtaia, in a patterned top and blue skirt, and her granddaughter, Anna, a younger woman in a white shirt and black skirt, sit on a woven mat, reminiscing over old photographs. Mangtaia holds up a photo of children, recalling a past where the sea was distant and the land greener, lamenting the current reality of living with rising waters. The scene shifts to a flooded residential area where homes are partially submerged, and a man wades through the water carrying a yellow bag, while another carries white containers, illustrating the daily disruption caused by the permanently flooded access road, which now necessitates boat travel. Anna, holding a framed photo of children in school uniforms, expresses concern for the future, noting that even high tides and storms cause water to spray over the sea wall, and voices fear of having to leave their home. The narrator emphasizes the increasing encroachment of the sea and the vulnerability of Kiribati's people, who are defenseless against monthly storm surges. The video contrasts serene beaches and children swimming at sunset with a stormy coastline featuring violent waves, a damaged boat, and a partially submerged house, underscoring nature's destructive power and the ongoing struggle against environmental change, culminating in a final aerial shot of the two women standing by a seawall, looking out at the turquoise water.

raw_segments: The video opens with an aerial view of a low-lying island in Kiribati, its fragile existence highlighted by turquoise waters encroaching on homes and vegetation, emphasizing the threat of sea-level rise, as a male narrator states this is a symbol of Kiribati's fight for survival. The scene transitions to a thatched hut where an older woman, Mangtaia, wearing a patterned top and blue skirt, and her granddaughter, Anna, a younger woman in a white shirt and black skirt, sit on a woven mat, examining old photographs together. The narrator explains they often reminisce about times before climate change affected them. Mangtaia holds up a photo of children and speaks in her native language, translated as, "20 years ago, the sea was far away. We were safe back then, even at high tide. The water only reached this far. We used to have a lot of trees. Our land was much greener. We had no idea we'd have to live like this one day." Close-ups reveal the photos depict children in school uniforms, evoking memories of a past life before the flooding. The camera then cuts to a flooded residential area, where homes are partially submerged, and a man wades through the water carrying a yellow bag, highlighting daily life amid rising waters. Another man is seen carrying white containers through the flooded street, underscoring the disruption to normal routines, while the narrator mentions the access road has been flooded for years, forcing the family to use a boat. The video returns to Mangtaia and Anna, now holding a framed photo of children. Anna speaks, her expression conveying concern and reflection, stating, "When I was little, life here was really good. There were lots of trees under which we played hide-and-seek. I've heard that other countries are causing climate change. But I don't hate those people." The sequence ends with a wide shot of the flooded landscape, reinforcing the environmental crisis and human resilience.

The segment opens with a wide shot of a flooded residential area, where homes are partially submerged in calm turquoise water amidst lush greenery and palm trees, with laundry hanging and tires forming a shoreline barrier. A soft, melancholic background music begins as an older female voice attributes the cause of such scenes to Western countries and climate change, stating, "I see it differently. The Western countries are doing many things to cause climate change. They're to blame. All I can do is ask them to stop." The camera pans to a thatched hut on a rocky base, partially surrounded by water, symbolizing vulnerability. A close-up then shows the Older woman in a patterned top and blue skirt, sitting under the hut on a woven mat, holding old photographs, while the Younger woman in a white shirt and black skirt sits beside her, holding a framed photograph of children in school uniforms, reflecting on lost childhoods. A male narrator's voice explains, "The sea is increasingly devouring unprotected tracts of land in the middle of the South Pacific." The scene transitions to a serene beach with a thatched hut and calm waters, followed by children swimming in shallow water at sunset. An aerial view reveals a low-lying island, identified as Kiribati, surrounded by a turquoise lagoon and deep blue ocean, emphasizing its fragility. A younger female voice expresses concern, "This isn't a safe place for the future. When we have a high tide and a big storm, the water smashes into the sea wall and sprays over the top." The two women are then seen walking along a sandy shore and standing by a rocky seawall, with the older woman placing a comforting hand on the younger's shoulder. The narrator adds that "During storm surges, which occur once a month these days, the people of Kiribati are completely defenseless against the forces of nature." The younger female voice continues, "I'm scared that the water will continue to rise. Maybe we'll have to leave here one day and live elsewhere." The video then shows a stormy coastline with violent waves, a damaged boat, and a partially submerged house, highlighting nature's destructive power. The final shot is an aerial view of a coastal home with a seawall, where the two women stand together, looking out at the turquoise water, underscoring their ongoing struggle against environmental change.

event_blocks.summary: Aerial views of Kiribati's fragile coastline reveal flooded residential areas and debris along the shoreline, juxtaposed with a dense green mangrove forest where roots are highlighted as natural protection against soil erosion. On a sandy beach at Ambo, during low tide, a group of local people, including climate activists like Vacity Tebamarae in a pink checkered top, engage in mangrove reforestation, planting saplings in neat rows. Vacity explains the planting strategy, emphasizing the need to plant multiple propagules per spot due to potential wave damage, while others sort and organize the green mangrove propagules. The narrator notes that over 50,000 mangroves have already been planted on the atoll, and young people, growing up with climate change, express fear for their island's future, with one young man stating, "Sea level rising is very important, a big impact to our island. I just don't want to see my island get drowned under the sea," and a young woman adding, "Yes, in my village where I live, most of the houses are destroyed by the sea level." The effort is described as a community-driven restoration under bright sunlight, transforming the beach into a young mangrove nursery. Later, a woman in a pink checkered top with 'JANICE' embroidered on her chest expresses pride in Kiribati despite facing challenges, and another man emphasizes belonging to the island due to culture and identity. Aerial shots showcase the expansive mangrove forest bordering the coastline, with the hope that these mangroves will grow into a forest to defy storm surges. In a different location, workers construct a sandbag barrier along the shore, filling and stacking sacks to form a protective wall, a national priority for Kiribati despite government funding limitations, underscored by the sound of shovels digging and scraping sand amidst tense music.

raw_segments: The segment opens with sweeping aerial views of Kiribati's low-lying coastal areas, set to a melancholic yet hopeful background score, showcasing turquoise waters bordering narrow strips of land with scattered homes and vegetation, highlighting the environment's fragility to sea-level rise. The camera glides over flooded residential zones where houses are partially submerged amidst mangroves and palm trees, with visible debris along the shoreline. The scene transitions to a dense green mangrove forest, followed by close-ups of mangrove roots in shallow water, as a male narrator explains that these roots make the tropical trees perfect protection against soil erosion. The perspective shifts to a wide aerial shot of a sandy, exposed shoreline with a small cluster of homes, illustrating the settlement's vulnerability. The video then moves to ground level, showing a group of local people on a sandy beach engaged in mangrove reforestation. The narrator introduces them as young people and climate activists, specifically identifying Vacity Tebamarae, who leads the effort to plant mangroves on the beach at Ambo during low tide. Vacity, in a pink checkered top, gestures and speaks in a local language, then translates, stating, "We'll plant 15 rows." Another voice adds that the mangroves should be planted "a hand's width apart." Two women crouch on the sand, sorting and organizing green mangrove propagules, while others stand nearby holding tools. The group works collaboratively, planting saplings in neat rows in the sand, demonstrating community-driven environmental restoration efforts under bright sunlight and clear skies, underscoring the active, hopeful nature of the work, which contrasts with the earlier scenes of environmental degradation. The narrator concludes by mentioning Vacity has been organizing a growing number of such volunteer planting events.

On a wide, sunlit sandy beach, a vibrant community-led mangrove reforestation effort is underway, with the sounds of activity and speech accompanying the visual. A Woman in a pink checkered top, identified by her clear, instructional voice, explains that the green, elongated mangrove propagules are "seeds from the mangroves" and details their planting strategy: "We take three in case 'cause when we we plant them, the waves will come and maybe two will be flooded away. At least one will stay and be and will live." She gestures and speaks while actively planting, coordinating a diverse group of local people in casual beachwear who crouch and kneel to insert the propagules into the wet sand in neat rows. Close-up shots emphasize hands carefully placing the seedlings, while others sort propagules from piles. A narrator's voice reveals that "the people of Tarawa have already planted more than 50,000 mangroves on the beaches of the atoll." The camera alternates between wide shots of the large-scale planting and intimate close-ups of focused faces. The narrator further explains that "the young people here have grown up with climate change. They're scared," leading to voices from the group expressing their concerns: a young man states, "Sea level rising is very important, a big impact to our island. I just don't want to see my island get drowned under the sea," followed by a young woman adding, "Yes, in my village where I live, most of the houses are destroyed by the sea level." The background features a calm shoreline and distant greenery under a clear sky, creating a peaceful yet purposeful atmosphere of environmental stewardship as the sandy beach gradually transforms into a young mangrove nursery.

The segment opens with a close-up of hands meticulously planting green mangrove propagules into wet sand, set against a backdrop of somber orchestral music and a narrator discussing young people's reluctance to leave their homes. The camera then pulls back to reveal a group of local people, including a Woman in a pink checkered top with 'JANICE' embroidered on her chest, actively planting saplings in organized rows across a wide sandy beach. She speaks with an accent, expressing, "I'm so proud to be a Kiribati. Even though what we face with, we're still proud of our country." Another man's voice, also with an accent, adds, "This is where we belong. It's our culture, our identities, that's why I think this is where our hearts belong." Aerial shots follow, showcasing the expansive, lush green mangrove forest bordering the coastline, contrasting with the newly planted areas, as the narrator explains the hope for these mangroves on Ambo Beach to grow into a forest that helps defy storm surges, accompanied by more hopeful orchestral music. The scene transitions to a different location where workers are constructing a sandbag barrier along the shore, filling large sacks with sand and stacking them to form a protective wall. A distinct bell/chime sound, followed by a low hum, precedes the narrator stating that protecting the coastline has become a national priority for Kiribati, despite the government lacking funds for long-term infrastructure. The environment is bright and sunny, with clear skies and palm trees, emphasizing the community’s proactive efforts, underscored by the sound of a shovel digging and scraping sand as workers continue their labor, with tense, dramatic music playing.

event_blocks.summary: On a sandy beach, workers, including a man in a blue sleeveless shirt and another in a brown shirt, actively construct a concrete seawall under a bright blue sky, pouring and smoothing concrete with shovels and buckets, while atmospheric background music plays. Itim Wemwe, a young entrepreneur from Taborio, explains his fear of climate change and the necessity of building the seawall high for self-protection, emphasizing the urgency as the sea is calm but storms are inevitable. Aerial footage reveals the narrow, low-lying island of Tarawa, home to 60,000 people, bordered by a lagoon and ocean, dotted with homes and vegetation. The scene transitions to a narrow village alleyway lined with thatched huts and makeshift structures, where a woman in a pink shirt passes a child playing near a counter, a young girl in a checkered shirt writes in a notebook, and a woman in a blue patterned top peels a root vegetable. Climate activist Vasiti notes that resources are increasingly sparse, leading to migration for education and work, resulting in close living conditions and the easy spread of diseases, exacerbated by overpopulation on Tarawa, a major problem alongside climate change. A young girl in an orange mesh top speaks to the camera, followed by children playing near a chain-link fence, as Vasiti highlights the government's limited resources to employ the youth. The narrative shifts to a dusty road with pedestrians, including a man carrying a child, as the average family size is five to six children and almost every square meter of land is built upon. A boy in red shorts picks up debris from a rocky area. The woman in the pink shirt is seen on a beach near a clothesline, as Vasiti concludes that sea level rise and land erosion are the main threats, and while people build seawalls, they are ultimately ineffective against the encroaching sea. The segment opens with a close-up of a clothesline on a beach, with waves crashing, and a female voice stating that the water will inevitably take the land. A flooded residential area with submerged houses and palm trees is shown, recalling Ioane Teitiota's 2014 bid to be the first climate change refugee, detailed in a newspaper article. Inside, Ioane, in a blue shirt, speaks earnestly while a young child draws, and a woman in a white shirt expresses fear of their land being washed away. Despite Ioane's asylum application in New Zealand being rejected on climate change grounds, leading to his family's deportation, their home country's situation worsened. Family photos are displayed, followed by a stormy coastline with violent waves, visually reinforcing the destructive impact of climate change on these vulnerable communities.

raw_segments: Under a bright blue sky, a long concrete seawall is actively being constructed on a sandy beach, accompanied by atmospheric background music. Workers, including a man in a blue sleeveless shirt and another in a brown shirt, are seen using shovels and buckets to pour and smooth concrete. A male voice, identified by the narrator as Itim Wemwe, a young entrepreneur from the village of Taborio, explains his motivation: "I think climate change will only get worse in the future. One day Kiribati will drown. Other people think differently, but I'm scared by what's happening. We have to protect ourselves. That's why we're building the seawall this high." The man in the blue shirt gestures as he speaks, emphasizing the urgency, while the camera pans to show the extent of the seawall stretching along the shore. The narrator adds that "The sea is calm today, but it's only a matter of time before the next storm surge arrives." A wider view of the beach reveals scattered debris and a small orange tent where more workers are mixing materials. Aerial footage then captures the narrow, low-lying island, bordered by a turquoise lagoon on one side and the deep blue ocean on the other, dotted with homes and vegetation. The narrator states, "Tarawa is home to around 60,000 people, more than half the country's entire population." The camera descends into a village, showing a narrow alleyway lined with thatched huts and makeshift structures, where a woman in a pink shirt walks past a child playing near a counter, highlighting daily life in this coastal community, with faint background chatter, possibly including a mention of "Liverpool FC," audible.

The video opens with a woman in a pink shirt and patterned shorts walking through a narrow, sunlit village alleyway, passing a child playing near a wooden counter where two women are seated – one in a white tank top beside 'Milk Powder' and 'Bisleri', and another in a blue patterned top peeling a root vegetable into a light blue basin. A young girl in a checkered shirt writes in a notebook on the ground, as a male voice notes that resources are "increasingly sparse," a sentiment echoed by climate activist Vasiti, who explains that "Compared to 10 years ago, in islands like this, people stayed in their homelands. But nowadays, people migrate from there for a better future. They migrate for education, for work, for everything. Yeah, and now it ended up like this. Everyone lives closely to each other." The scene shifts to a wider village view, showing a woman in a green top near a wooden structure and a black pig foraging among debris near a rusted metal wall, while the male voice adds that "In addition to climate change, overpopulation is one of the biggest problems on Tarawa." A young girl in an orange mesh top then speaks directly to the camera, followed by children playing near a chain-link fence, as Vasiti continues, "Diseases easily pass from one person to another. And especially with the future of the kids, the government have a limited resource. It can't employ each and every one of them." The setting transitions to a dusty road with vehicles and pedestrians, including a woman in a white dress and a man carrying a child, while the male voice states, "On average, a family here has five to six children. Almost every square meter of land on Tarawa has now been built on." A boy in red shorts picks up debris from a rocky, littered area. The final shot shows the woman in the pink shirt on a sandy beach, gesturing near a makeshift clothesline with laundry, ocean, and scattered debris in the background, as Vasiti concludes, "The main thing that is threatening us is the sea level rise. We have land erosion. The sea is taking most of the lands and people here try whatever they have to build seawalls. But it doesn't help when the water comes..." The overall atmosphere depicts daily life in a resource-limited, coastal community facing resilience and environmental challenges, underscored by the urgent commentary.

The segment opens with a close-up of a makeshift clothesline on a sandy beach, colorful garments swaying as ocean waves crash, while a female voice states, "no one can stop the water, it just comes and then take away all the lands again." The scene transitions to a flooded residential area with a partially submerged house and swaying palm trees, as a calm male narrator explains that in 2014, global warming's effects on Kiribati, specifically Ioane Teitiota's bid to become the world's first climate change refugee, attracted international attention. A newspaper article titled 'Klimaflucht aus der Südsee' (Climate Flight from the South Pacific) is displayed, detailing Ioane's asylum application in New Zealand. Indoors, a man in a blue shirt, identified as Ioane, sits on a mat speaking earnestly, uttering "Sa ku kaiti" in a local language, while a young child draws beside him. A woman in a white shirt gestures as she speaks, later heard saying "Ma koronte okanati" and expressing fear that their low-lying land is "being washed away around us." The narrator continues, explaining that New Zealand didn't recognize climate change as grounds for asylum, leading to Ioane and his family's deportation a year later, despite their home country's worsening situation, which was "a shock for the family." A series of family photos, including Ioane holding a baby, are shown, emphasizing their personal story. The segment concludes with a stormy coastline, violent waves crashing against homes and palm trees, visually reinforcing the destructive force of climate change on these vulnerable island communities, as the woman's emotional voice underscores the threat.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a modest home, a woman in a white patterned top and a man in a blue shirt, seated with a young child on a patterned mat, express deep concerns about their future and safety on Kiribati amidst melancholic background music. The woman, speaking earnestly, voices her primary worry for her children's safety, stating, 'My biggest worry is my children. I want them to be safe. We don't want to wait until the end.' The man, appearing concerned, addresses criticism for seeking climate change refuge, explaining, 'Many people criticize me for trying to be a climate change refugee, saying I'm talking my country down. But I want to leave again. It doesn't matter where we live. Our culture will survive with us.' The scene shifts to two young girls playing with colorful plastic toys on a tiled floor, followed by children wading through shallow outdoor floodwaters, their laughter and shouts audible. As the narrator provides historical context about Kiribati's inhabitants and traditions, a group of young people in traditional attire, adorned with leafy necklaces, woven skirts, and shell headpieces, prepare for a cultural performance in a covered outdoor area. The sounds of children's play briefly continue before rhythmic chanting, drum beats, and synchronized vocalizations commence, accompanying the traditional dance group as they perform a synchronized traditional dance on a blue floor under a corrugated roof, with a decorated altar in the background, highlighting communal cultural expression and pride.

Kabuwa John, a man in a gray t-shirt and blue shorts, walks through a dry, cracked landscape, pointing to the horizon and expressing concern about land degradation, lamenting the past state of the land before its current condition. Kaboa reappears, walking through the desiccated terrain, expressing urgency about his initial dismissal of climate change and his current deep worry over its profound impacts, contemplating migration with his two children to Australia or New Zealand due to increasing sea-level rise, despite his desire to remain. event_blocks.summary: Aerial drone footage reveals a vast, dry, cracked landscape of an exposed seabed near Kiribati, a stark contrast to the lush palm trees and thatched homes lining a turquoise lagoon. Kaboa, wearing a gray polo shirt with 'QBE' and Puma logos, expresses deep concern about climate change and cultural preservation, stating, "We want to die here. We don't want to go to overseas. We want to stay here. When we move, our culture will be disappeared." He walks through a village, where many are unemployed, to demonstrate palm sugar production funded by the government. He passes a man in a yellow tank top holding a baby in a pink hat, and a shirtless man in a green sarong, observing daily village life. Kaboa seeks a worker named Takamba, who is then shown skillfully climbing a 20-meter coconut palm to collect sap, attaching a yellow plastic bag to one flower and a clear plastic bottle to another. After descending, Takamba hands the sap to a man in a black shirt. Kaboa joins a communal cooking area under a thatched shelter, where women are preparing food. A woman in a green and white checkered top tends a wok over an open fire, while another in an orange top and green skirt stirs its contents. Kaboa pours the collected Toddy into the wok, explaining, "This is the Toddy, fresh one" and "Water," as it begins to boil and steam vigorously. The narrator explains Kaboa learned palm sugar making in Thailand with government funding, and that locals have only used fresh Toddy until now. Kaboa further explains the process of crystallizing the Toddy into sugar, noting that "In Kiribati, we only have the, you know, copra, copra cutting as a source of income. So, our government tried to find ways which can help people like to earn more money, and this is one of the income for the farmers." It takes 7 liters of sap to produce 1 kilogram of palm sugar. The Man in gray t-shirt instructs that the fire must not be too hot and the sap must be stirred constantly until it becomes hard and powdery, with the "palm sugar from Abaiang" set to be sold for 5 euros per kilo. A woman in a green and white checkered top states they will continue this work to earn money and feed their families. The Man in gray t-shirt shows the group a bottle of sap before passing it to the Man in black shirt. The group, including a woman in an orange top and green skirt, actively stirs the sap. The atmosphere is communal and warm, with laughter and conversation. The Man in gray t-shirt tastes the sap, smiling, as a male voice comments, "This one is pretty good." He explains the government plans to export the palm sugar globally, with contacts secured at the World's Fair in Milan in 2015, and shows what the finished sugar looks like. The rustic, sunlit environment highlights the communal effort and cultural tradition of making coconut syrup, with an older shirtless man in blue jeans and a young boy in a yellow shirt observing.

raw_segments: The video opens with sweeping aerial drone footage of a vast, dry, cracked landscape, appearing as a once-submerged coastal area now exposed due to low tide or environmental change. This barren terrain, textured with patterns of dried mud and coral, stretches towards a line of lush green palm trees and traditional thatched-roof homes along the edge of a turquoise lagoon. Against a backdrop of ambient music, Kaboa, wearing a gray polo shirt with a 'QBE' logo and a Puma emblem, stands in this desolate expanse, gesturing expressively as he speaks with deep concern, stating, "We are from Kiribati. We are proud to be Kiribati. We want to die here. We don't want to go to overseas. We want to stay here. When we move, our culture will be disappeared." The camera alternates between wide aerial views and closer shots of him, emphasizing the scale of the exposed seabed. The scene transitions to a village setting, where Kaboa walks through a narrow dirt path lined with thatched huts, coconut trees, and scattered household items. A narrator explains that there are many people on Abaiang without work, and Kaboa is there on behalf of the government to show people how to make palm sugar. As Kaboa passes a man in a yellow tank top holding a baby in a pink hat, and a shirtless man in a green sarong leaning against a hut, the rustic, tropical environment shows visible signs of daily life, including cooking areas, laundry hanging, and children playing. Kaboa continues walking, engaging briefly with an older, shirtless man in blue jeans who gestures toward him, while dialogue in a foreign language is heard, with Kaboa specifically looking for a worker named Takamba. The atmosphere conveys quiet resilience amid environmental stress, with the exposed seabed and village life highlighting the vulnerability of this coastal community to climate change.

The segment opens with the sounds of local speech as Takamaba, identified by the narrator, skillfully ascends a 20-meter tall coconut palm tree, gripping the trunk amidst lush green fronds to collect sap, known locally as Toddy. He first attaches a yellow plastic bag to a coconut flower, then a clear plastic bottle to another. The camera cuts to Kaboah, wearing a gray polo shirt with 'QBE' and Puma logos, looking upward, observing Takamaba's work. After descending, Takamaba walks toward a thatched hut area, handing the collected sap to a man in a black shirt, and the two walk together past a white bucket and another thatched structure. The scene transitions to a communal cooking area under a thatched shelter, where women are preparing food. A woman in a green and white checkered top tends a large wok over an open fire fueled by dried palm fronds, while another in an orange top and green skirt stirs the contents. Kaboah, who the narrator explains learned palm sugar making in Thailand with funding from the Kiribati government, joins them, pouring the collected Toddy into the wok, stating "This is the Toddy, fresh one" and "Water" as the liquid splashes and begins to boil and steam vigorously. The group gathers around the fire, with others seated nearby, as the sap is cooked down, creating a traditional, warm atmosphere under natural light filtering through the roof, while the narrator notes that up until now, locals have only used the fresh Toddy.

The video opens with a close-up of a smiling woman under a thatched shelter, transitioning to a wider shot of a group gathered around an open fire where a large wok is placed. A Man in gray t-shirt, wearing a 'QBE' logo and Puma emblem, actively pours coconut sap from a clear plastic bottle into the heated wok, explaining, "No, we're going to boil the fresh water, the fresh toddy... so that we can crystallize the toddy, become sugar, you know, the solid part." He is assisted by a Man in black shirt, while a Woman in green and white checkered top and a Woman in orange top and green skirt tend to the wok, stirring the sap as it boils and thickens. Upbeat, slightly exotic background music begins as the scene briefly cuts to sweeping aerial views of a lush, tropical island bordered by a turquoise lagoon and deep blue ocean. A narrator's voice identifies this as "The island of Abaiang," stating it's "the perfect place for producing palm sugar," with "thousands of coconut palms across the 37-kilometer island." Returning to the cooking group, the Man in gray t-shirt, with an animated expression, further explains, "In Kiribati, we only have the, you know, copra, copra cutting as a source of income. So, our government tried to find ways which can help people like to earn more money, and this is one of the income for the farmers." The camera focuses on the wok, showing the sap bubbling and being stirred with a wooden paddle, indicating its transformation. The narrator adds, "It takes 7 liters of sap to produce 1 kilogram of palm sugar," emphasizing the traditional, sustainable lifestyle in this rustic, natural environment under the thatched hut.

The video opens with a close-up of the Man in gray t-shirt, animatedly speaking to the camera under a thatched shelter, explaining that the fire mustn't be too hot and one must stir the "toady" (coconut sap) constantly, pushing it up the side until it becomes hard and powdery. The scene quickly shifts to a wider view showing him crouched beside a large wok over an open fire, stirring the thick, bubbling coconut sap with a wooden paddle. Smoke rises from the fire, fueled by dried palm fronds, as he works alongside other men and children seated on the ground, observing or assisting. The audio reveals that this "palm sugar from Abaiang" is set to be sold for 5 euros per kilo. The camera captures close-ups of the wok, emphasizing the boiling sap and the steam rising from it, accompanied by the sounds of stirring, fire crackling, and background conversation and laughter. A Woman in green and white checkered top, actively stirring the sap, states that they will continue this work to earn money and feed their families. The Man in gray t-shirt then holds up a clear plastic bottle filled with coconut sap, showing it to the group, before passing it to the Man in black shirt, who takes it and walks away. The group, including the Woman in green and white checkered top and another Woman in an orange top and green skirt, actively participates in stirring the sap. The atmosphere is communal and warm, with laughter and conversation filling the space. The camera pans to show the broader setting: a woven mat under the thatched hut, with several men and children seated around, some barefoot, others in simple clothing, all engaged in the cooking process. The Man in gray t-shirt is later seen tasting the sap from his finger, smiling, with a male voice commenting, "This one is pretty good," while others watch. The scene concludes with a wider shot of the group, including an Older shirtless man in blue jeans, gesturing toward the cooking area, and a Young boy in a yellow shirt sitting nearby, as the Man in gray t-shirt continues to explain the process, holding the bottle of sap, mentioning that the government plans to export the palm sugar from Abaiang around the world, with contacts secured at the World's Fair in Milan in 2015, and showing what the sugar from the palm sap looks like. The environment is rustic and sunlit, with natural light filtering through the thatched roof, highlighting the communal effort and cultural tradition of making coconut syrup.

event_blocks.summary: In a rustic, open-air thatched shelter, a man in a gray polo shirt offers a granular substance, possibly coconut sap or sugar, to an older shirtless man in blue jeans, inquiring about its taste and quality, to which a voice from the group responds positively, comparing it to candy. The scene then shifts to a covered pavilion with a corrugated roof where women and children are preparing food; a woman with curly red hair, wearing glasses and green leaves in her hair, demonstrates cutting a large green squash, explaining the edible and medicinal properties of its parts and surrounding herbs, emphasizing that "You're not just making a meal, you are also making medicine." She also discusses the nutritional value of squash and the importance of using local herbs. Tonga Fiti Cross, a women's rights activist and nutritionist, leads a cooking class, demonstrating how to prepare traditional Kiribati recipes using local ingredients like palm leaves for wrapping fish and herbs for soup, while also frying vegetables and eggs in a wok over a portable gas stove. The narrator highlights that these ingredients are found in the jungle and that Tonga Fiti's mission is to share this knowledge. The communal atmosphere is evident as women laugh and share fritters, and peel vegetables, with a narrator explaining the shift from traditional diets to imported foods leading to health issues like obesity and diabetes in Kiribati. Separately, inside a traditional 'Maneaba' or community center, village representatives, including climate activist Vasiti (an older woman in a colorful floral top), are engaged in a community planning session focused on securing potable water supplies, using white cards with handwritten notes and a large white sheet on the floor covered with red sticky notes organized into 'To be done,' 'In progress,' and 'Done' columns. They discuss challenges like salinization of wells and the need for solutions such as solar pumps, hand pumps, and desalination plants, with a man in a beige polo shirt pointing to a large white board with yellow sticky notes to finalize criteria. The overall narrative emphasizes the preservation of traditional knowledge, community collaboration, and efforts to address health and environmental challenges in a lush, tropical setting.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a gray polo shirt with a 'QBE' logo and a Puma emblem, holding a clear plastic bag filled with a granular substance, possibly coconut sap or sugar. He interacts with a shirtless older man in blue jeans, seated on a woven mat in a rustic, open-air thatched shelter, asking, "Do you want to try it?" and later, "What do you think of the new sugar? It's not bad, right?" and "Do you like it?". A voice from the group responds that "It tastes like candy. This sugar's really different, much more delicious." Several other men, some shirtless and others in casual t-shirts, including one in a yellow t-shirt and another in a blue patterned sarong, are gathered, engaging in conversation and examining the bag's contents. The camera then pans to a wider view of the group before transitioning to a different area under a covered pavilion with a corrugated roof, where a group of women and children are preparing food. A woman with curly red hair, wearing a white t-shirt with a blue stripe and a patterned skirt, handles a large green squash, while others peel vegetables and prepare ingredients on a white cloth spread on the floor. Children are present, some observing, others actively participating, including one in a pink dress playing with toys and another in a striped shirt handling vegetables. The atmosphere is communal and focused on food preparation, with natural light filtering through the open sides of the structure. Background music begins, and a narrator explains that "Traditional Kiribati recipes are increasingly fading from memory." Women's rights activist and nutritionist, Tonga Fiti Cross, is then introduced, stating her desire to "fight that trend" by teaching people "how to eat food and how to prepare food," emphasizing the use of "the leaves and the herbs that they have in their own country" which they "never use it because they don't know it, that they're edible." Close-ups show hands peeling vegetables, the large squash being cut, and a woman in a red shirt with 'Big B' printed on it smiling as she works with a long green vegetable. The environment is lush and tropical, with palm trees visible, conveying a mood of collaboration and daily life in a rural, coastal community.

The video segment opens with a close-up of a woman in a blue floral top, her hair tied back with a red band, focused on a task. The scene transitions to a wider view under a covered pavilion with a corrugated roof, where a group of women are seated on a woven bamboo floor, preparing food together. The central figure, a woman with curly red hair wearing a white t-shirt and a patterned skirt, is actively demonstrating how to cut and prepare a large green squash using a knife. She wears glasses and has green leaves tucked into her hair, adding a natural, cultural touch. As she works, a woman's voice, likely hers, explains, "You can use these palm leaves in many different ways, not just to swat flies. We'll wrap them around the fish. Rip the leaves into two. When you cook these leaves, a liquid comes out that's very good for the stomach." She continues, demonstrating, "You fold the leaves like this, and wrap the fish." Other women, including one in a red shirt with 'Big B' printed on it and another in a blue floral top, are engaged in similar tasks, handling long green vegetables and peeling them with care. A male voice then adds historical context, stating, "Fish has been prepared like this on Kiribati for generations." The white cloth on the floor is spread out with various ingredients, including eggs in a carton and fresh produce. The atmosphere is communal and collaborative, with the women conversing and smiling as they work. In the background, a woman in a yellow tank top holds a baby, while children sit nearby, observing or playing. The woman's voice then returns, discussing the shift from traditional "right food" to imported food, lamenting, "But then when they imported food comes, and that's where things has gone wrong. Because in in in there's the hospital is full of people, the sick people. And we tried to cut down there, the people to sick, you know, and receive the right food so cut down there um lack of vitamins in there in their food and not." The setting is bright and open, with natural light filtering through the structure, and tropical greenery visible outside, emphasizing a traditional, rural environment. The women’s clothing is colorful and casual, reflecting local styles, and their actions are deliberate and skilled, suggesting a shared cultural practice or preparation for a communal meal.

The video opens with a close-up of the Woman with curly red hair, wearing a white t-shirt with a blue stripe and a patterned skirt, her glasses reflecting light and green leaves tucked into her hair, as she animatedly speaks about food preparation, stating that certain foods "can't prevent any disease" but then elaborating on the nutritional value of squash. She explains that "You can eat every part of the squash: the flower, the stem, the skin, the seeds," and advises to cut slices thinly. She then highlights the medicinal properties of herbs, asking, "Why do we want a lot of herbs in our soup?" and answering that "All these herbs have medicinal properties. Your body's take in these herbs and you get healthier," concluding, "You're not just making a meal, you are also making medicine." The scene transitions to a close-up of a large green squash being cut with a knife on a wooden board, surrounded by green leaves and a checkered cloth, illustrating her instructions. The camera then pulls back to reveal a group of women and children seated on a white cloth under a covered pavilion with a corrugated roof, engaged in preparing food. The Woman with curly red hair leads the activity, gesturing as she speaks, while others, including the Woman in red shirt (wearing a red shirt with 'Big B' printed on it), the Woman in blue floral top (in a blue floral top and pink skirt), and the Woman in yellow tank top (holding a baby wrapped in a blue cloth, the baby wearing a pink hat), peel and chop vegetables. The group works collaboratively, with some handling long green vegetables and others slicing squash. The setting is bright and natural, with sunlight filtering through the open sides of the pavilion and tropical greenery visible in the background. The scene shifts to a large pot on a portable stove, where a woman stirs a simmering mixture of yellow squash and green leaves, adding more greens from a blue container. As the camera pans to show the full group seated in a circle, continuing their food preparation with a carton of eggs and various tools laid out on the white cloth, a male voice narrates, providing context that "Two-thirds of Kiribati's population consumes a staple diet of fish and imported rice, a diet increasingly supplemented with high sugar foods, leading to rising rates of obesity and diabetes." The atmosphere is communal and focused, with the women engaged in conversation and coordinated effort, culminating in a close-up of the Woman in red shirt smiling as she works, highlighting the warmth and cooperation of the gathering.

The video opens with a woman in a red shirt, expressing gratitude for the cooking class led by nutritionist Tonga Fiti Cross, stating she now knows more about cooking and plans to use many herbs and vegetables for her daughter's health. In the background, Tonga Fiti, with curly red hair and green leaves in her hair, stirs a large pot of vibrant green leaves, orange squash, and white coconut pieces. Another woman's voice expresses surprise, noting they previously only used these herbs for costumes and didn't know they could be cooked. The scene shifts to Tonga Fiti, now wearing glasses, frying a mixture of vegetables and eggs in a wok over a portable gas stove, creating a distinct sizzling sound. A narrator highlights that all the ingredients Tonga Fiti demonstrates can be found in the jungle, emphasizing her mission to share knowledge across Abaiang. The camera captures a woman in a purple shirt laughing as golden-brown fritters are handed around, and a woman in a blue floral top peels a long green vegetable, all under a covered pavilion. The setting then transitions to exterior shots of a large thatched hut, identified by the narrator as a 'Maneaba' – a traditional community center in Kiribati that provides shade and protection. Inside this Maneaba, a group sits on woven mats, engaged in a discussion or activity involving papers and cards, with a woman in a yellow floral dress speaking, fostering a communal and collaborative atmosphere, accompanied by upbeat, traditional-sounding background music.

The scene opens inside a thatched hut, where women, including a woman in a blue shirt and an older woman with gray hair in a colorful floral top, are seated on woven mats, holding white cards with handwritten text like 'Safe Well Water', 'R hand Pump', and 'Communal Rain Tank', indicating a community planning session. A gentle, rhythmic background music plays as a voice explains that representatives from several villages are seeking potable water supplies. A female voice emphasizes the importance of fresh, clean water for everyone to live longer and healthier lives. The camera pans across the group, showing more participants, including men, engaged in conversation around a large white sheet on the floor covered with red sticky notes arranged under 'To be done', 'In progress', and 'Done' columns, suggesting project tracking. A narrator then identifies the older woman in the colorful floral top as climate activist Vasiti, who is working to combat the salinization of drinking water. Close-ups show individuals speaking earnestly, with one female voice describing how seawater sometimes flows into her well, flooding it with dirt and leaving no water to drink. Another male voice laments that their groundwater, once fresh and clean, is now salty and unpalatable. The scene transitions outdoors, where a man in a purple sarong is seen working with a shovel near a concrete structure, possibly a water tank or foundation, surrounded by construction materials, hinting at ongoing solutions. Back in the hut, discussions continue, with another male voice explaining how wells near the sea are regularly flooded and spoiled by salt, making the water feel like seawater if used for washing. The video shows a close-up of a large white plastic container with a blue lid and a white ribbon, a potential water storage vessel. The final frames show the woman in a blue floral top and the older woman (Vasiti) in a colorful patterned top seated together, engaged in conversation, with Vasiti wiping her face, reflecting the emotional weight of their water security and resilience planning efforts in this natural, communal setting.

Underpinned by uplifting, rhythmic music, a community planning session unfolds inside a spacious, open-sided thatched hut with a woven bamboo floor, where village representatives, including men and women of various ages, are seated in a semi-circle, actively addressing the challenge of supplying clean drinking water. A narrator introduces the problem and the presentation of solutions. A woman in a yellow floral dress, standing and speaking to the group, proposes a solar pump, detailing the need for equipment like pipes to pump well water to houses. Following her, a man in a light green polo shirt, holding a white board with red sticky notes, presents a desalination plant as the optimal solution for everyone. Another woman, wearing a purple shirt and holding a white card with 'R hand Pump' written on it, suggests the necessity of a hand pump for water supply. The narrator emphasizes the importance of cheap and long-lasting solutions. Participants, including a woman in a blue top and a woman in a red and blue patterned skirt, sit cross-legged, observing and occasionally contributing, while a woman in a floral top stands and listens intently. The atmosphere is participatory and focused, with papers, markers, and cards laid out on the floor, displaying categories like 'To be done,' 'In progress,' and 'Done,' indicating a structured project management initiative. Finally, a man in a beige polo shirt, standing and pointing at a large white board with yellow sticky notes, announces the plan to lay out all criteria on the floor, reflecting a dynamic and inclusive decision-making process.

event_blocks.summary: The process of community engagement begins with a close-up of papers marked with red dots and local language text on a textured floor, where a hand applies a red dot, signifying a vote, a crucial step for village delegates as explained by Robert K., an Australian development aid worker. In a thatched-roof communal space, diverse local participants, including men and women in colorful attire, gather around the papers, with Robert K. clarifying that the exercise encourages critical thinking about aid rather than immediate project selection, addressing Kiribati's dependency issues. A man in a red 'REPUBLIC' t-shirt guides the activity, while participants seated on woven mats engage in writing and discussion. Pelenisa Alofa, founder of the environmental protection organization Kirikan, visually identified as a woman in a yellow floral dress with a white flower, emphasizes water as Kiribati's priority need, explaining the deployment of water tanks for rain harvesting, a focus reinforced by individuals holding cards labeled 'Safe Well Water' and 'To hand pump.' Outdoor scenes reveal a green water tank under a shelter and a solar panel array near a thatched hut, part of sustainable development efforts, with the narrator noting that each family will receive a water tank soon. The narrative shifts to a serene beach with a stilted thatched hut featuring two solar panels, part of an EU-funded 'SOLAR ENERGY PROJECT' for outer islands. The woman in the yellow floral dress passionately addresses the group, stressing the urgency of adaptation to prevent displacement and preserve culture, stating, "People will be forced to leave if we do not step in, right now, today, to help them to adapt, to stay. This is what adaptation is all about, for people to remain in their own homes, in their own... continue with their life and their culture." She further articulates the deep connection to land and the fear of becoming a "second class person" in a foreign land, accompanied by subtle, emotional music. Visuals transition to a golden sunset over the ocean, silhouetting palm trees and thatched huts, followed by a woman cleaning fish on pebble-covered ground with children playing nearby, a colonial-style church with a clock tower, and the woman in the yellow floral dress reappearing. event_blocks.summary: The Marine Training Centre, located on the atoll of Tarawa and identified by its sign and motto 'STEERING STANDARD IN THE RIGHT DIRECTION', trains up to 150 students annually to become seafarers, ship mechanics, and cooks, serving as a "window of opportunity to a better life for many young Kiribars." Trainees in grey t-shirts and numbered tags, such as '9619', retrieve items from lockers in a covered walkway, with a bell and whistle signaling a shift to an outdoor courtyard. There, instructors in white shirts, some marked 'MTC TARAWA', conduct a strict morning roll call with trainees standing at attention, hands behind their backs, verifying identities with clipboards and noting absences with punishments like "one hour each," followed by a collective "sir!" from the trainees. Later, under a covered training area labeled 'TERAAKA BETIO,' trainees in orange hard hats and blue overalls actively handle large coiled ropes and mooring equipment, responding to precise commands from an instructor in a white hard hat, such as 'Seven people at the stern and the rest at the spring' and 'Let's go, spring first!', accompanied by the sounds of ropes being pulled and equipment moving. The narrator highlights the demand for Kiribati seafarers, especially on German vessels, with six Hamburg shipping companies having offices on Tarawa, supervised by Andrew Heinsen, who observes that islanders have a strong connection to water from childhood fishing. The demanding 15-month process requires trainees to be on time, clean, and well-dressed, facing a "completely different world" and working hard for their dream. In a classroom setting, trainees study textbooks like 'KIRIBATI FISHING MASTER' and 'ENGINEERING AND DESIGN,' with one trainee examining a diagram of knots. Trainee Tamuerara, who sees his family only on weekends, shares a dorm room with 19 other sailors. Trainee '9619' walks through a covered walkway with lockers and enters a dormitory with multiple wooden bunk beds and woven mats, making his bed on bunk bed '11' with a pillow and blanket, and describing his mattress made of palm leaves, which he rolls out each night to sleep on, illustrating the structured, communal living environment.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a dormitory at the Marine Training Centre, a trainee wearing a grey t-shirt with the number '9615' on it, initially lies on a woven mat atop a wooden bunk bed before sitting up to address the camera. Gesturing with his hands, he explains in English his motivation for training as a merchant marine, citing his desire to provide for his family and his aspiration to pursue further training post-program. The dormitory is furnished with multiple bunk beds, a purple door, and a small shelf. The scene shifts to an outdoor courtyard featuring a large white sailor statue, identified with 'MTC' on its cap, positioned on a rocky base and enclosed by a chain barrier, adjacent to a building labeled '15'. A sign on a green wall indicates 'DUTY OFFICE' and displays a German flag alongside text confirming it is 'FUNDED BY THE GERMAN GOVERNMENT'. As gentle background music commences, a narrator comments on the training center's positive reputation in Kiribati, while also noting that the modern facility is increasingly affected by flooding. Transitioning to a coastal perspective from an elevated platform, a man in a black polo shirt and beige shorts observes and points towards a tranquil, greenish lagoon fringed by a sandy beach and palm trees. He explains in German, translated for the audience, that the basin's entrance is obstructed by sand, accumulated overnight due to a breach in the seawall caused by unusually high water levels and inadequate upkeep. The water is visibly laden with floating debris, and a rusted car door lies half-submerged among rocks, underscoring the vulnerability of the coastal environment as the camera pans across a rocky shoreline with coral formations, revealing a solitary figure walking in the distance.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a black polo shirt stands on a raised platform by a lagoon and rocky shoreline, pointing towards the water and explaining how currents deposit sand. Aerial views reveal a long causeway across turquoise waters, with vehicles moving on its surface, while the narrator notes restricted water exchange and human interference with nature leading to disastrous consequences, describing the causeway as a costly, never-ending construction site. The scene shifts to a primary school in Bonriki, where children in yellow shirts and green skirts, some barefoot, gather for a morning prayer, with over 90% of Kiribati's population being Christian. Inside the classroom, a teacher in a white shirt and dark skirt stands near a chalkboard displaying 'Global Warming' with branches for 'Sea Level Rise' and 'Drought'. The teacher introduces the topic of climate change, and a girl defines it as a change in weather patterns over a long period. The children chant the effects of global warming from a poster: 'Sea level rise! Drought! Heavy rain!'. The teacher explains the importance of understanding climate change and how to deal with it, noting the children's fear of it. Students work on projects illustrating 'Land Erosion' and 'Sea Level Rise', drawing and writing about palm trees, houses, and flooding. The teacher advises on preventing soil erosion, building sea walls, planting mangroves, and not cutting down trees. Three students present a poster depicting land submerging and coastal erosion, with a boy stating, 'Our beaches will be washed away,' and 'We're using too much fuel.' The class applauds, and the teacher emphasizes climate change adaptation. The chalkboard later shows a close-up of 'Sea Level Rise in Kiribati'.

Their chatter and applause are heard.

raw_segments: The video opens with a wide shot of a rocky shoreline and a calm, greenish lagoon where a man in a black polo shirt and beige shorts stands on a raised platform, pointing toward the water and distant shoreline with a serious expression. A male narrator, initially speaking German, then translates, explaining how "the current deposited the sand here" and "has piled up here." Gentle, atmospheric background music begins as sweeping aerial views reveal a long, narrow causeway stretching across turquoise waters, with vehicles like trucks and motorcycles moving along its surface. The narrator comments, "People mess about with everything, just like they do in Europe," and notes that "driving along this causeway you can see that the water exchange is restricted to just a small aperture. All the water from the lagoon has to squeeze through it." The causeway appears weathered, with visible erosion and waves crashing against its sides, as the narrator continues, describing "human interference with nature, with disastrous consequences," explaining it "changed ocean currents" and that "the causeway is a costly, never-ending construction site." The scene shifts to ground-level shots of the causeway, showing a dusty, unpaved road where a motorcycle kicks up dust as it speeds by, accompanied by a distinct bell sound, followed by traditional music featuring a bell-like percussion. The atmosphere transitions to a school setting where children in yellow shirts and green skirts, some barefoot and carrying backpacks, walk along a dusty path. The narrator identifies this as "the primary school in Bonriki," stating that "every morning begins with a prayer," and that "more than 90% of Kiribati's population is Christian." The children gather in a large group on the ground, sitting in rows under a green, elevated school building, supervised by a teacher in a white shirt and dark skirt, and are heard chanting a prayer in a local language. They then enter the school building in an orderly fashion, their chanting voices continuing briefly before fading out.

The scene opens with a group of children, many barefoot and carrying backpacks, dressed in yellow shirts and green skirts, entering a green, elevated school building on stilts, suggesting a rural or coastal setting. Inside the modest classroom, illuminated by natural light through louvered windows, children sit cross-legged at wooden desks. A teacher in a white shirt and dark skirt stands near a chalkboard, which displays a diagram on 'Global Warming' with branches for 'Sea Level Rise' and 'Drought'. The teacher greets them with a clear, engaging voice, asking, "Good morning boys and girls!", to which the children enthusiastically respond, "Good morning teacher!". She then introduces the topic, and the children excitedly shout, "Climate change!". The teacher asks, "What is the meaning of this word, climate change?", and a girl articulately explains, "The change in weather pattern over a long period of time." The teacher confirms her answer, asking "Is she right?" and the children loudly affirm, "Yes!". She continues, explaining, "Now, these are the three causes caused by global warming," as she holds up a green poster titled 'Climate Change and its Effects'. The children, focused and interactive, chant the effects listed on the poster: "Sea level rise! Drought! Heavy rain!". The classroom is decorated with educational materials, including a world map and a colorful flag, reinforcing the dynamic, hands-on learning environment. Upbeat, traditional-sounding background music begins as a narrator poses the question, "So, what does the future hold for Kiribati's children? It's a subject addressed in primary schools," emphasizing the importance of this environmental education.

The video opens in a modest, sunlit classroom with wooden desks and louvered windows, where a teacher in a white blouse with lace detailing and a dark skirt stands addressing the camera. Ambient music plays softly as the teacher's voice explains the importance of understanding "what's going on here and how they can deal with the problem," noting that "Most of the children are scared of climate change." Students in yellow shirts and green skirts sit cross-legged on the floor, diligently working on climate change projects, their quiet chatter audible. A narrator adds that while the children's fears are concrete, their teacher is also "scared of the future too." The children, focused and engaged, draw and write on large sheets of paper, illustrating concepts like 'Land Erosion' and 'Sea Level Rise' with simple sketches of palm trees, houses, and flooding. One student, a girl with braided hair, carefully draws a palm tree near water, while another writes 'Climate Change and its Impacts' at the top of her sheet. The teacher's voice returns, emphasizing, "We can emigrate to another country or confront the problem and live with it. I always tell my students that we have to find ways of preventing soil erosion. We have to build sea walls and plant mangroves. We mustn't cut down our trees anymore." The camera pans across the room, capturing the collaborative atmosphere as students work individually and in pairs, drawing pictures to illustrate what they think will happen as a result of global warming. Later, three students stand at the front, proudly presenting their completed poster, which includes hand-drawn scenes of land submerging and coastal erosion, with handwritten text in English. A boy's voice is heard, relayed by the narrator, stating, "Our beaches will be washed away," and adding, "We're using too much fuel." The class applauds enthusiastically, and the presenters smile with pride. The teacher continues to speak, gesturing toward the students, reinforcing the educational focus on climate change adaptation. The classroom walls are decorated with educational materials, including a world map and a colorful flag, and natural light streams in, creating a warm, inviting atmosphere. The chalkboard in the background displays a diagram titled 'Global Warming' with branches labeled 'Sea Level Rise' and 'Drought', and later a close-up reveals 'Sea Level Rise in Kiribati', emphasizing the local relevance of the lesson.

event_blocks.summary: In a Kiribati classroom, children present a hand-drawn poster illustrating sea-level rise, with one girl articulating the fear of drowning if the ocean continues to encroach. The teacher, standing by a chalkboard and an educational poster on climate change, oversees the lesson where students, seated cross-legged, listen intently. A child recounts her home being flooded and her mother carrying her to safety, while another expresses helplessness against climate change. Melancholic music accompanies these reflections before an aerial view of Kiribati's fragile island transitions to ground-level shots of children wading through flooded streets and a boy cycling through shallow water. The narrator explains the village of Bonriki's increased susceptibility to prolonged flooding. The scene then shifts to Tamuera, a trainee seafarer, in a lush yard with a thatched hut, stating his intention to buy a house and raise his family there after completing his marine training and securing employment on a ship. He begins digging with a hoe, planning to cultivate a garden with sweet potatoes, tapioca, and lettuce, and to build a fence. Meanwhile, another man in a red t-shirt adjusts a large, rusted metal object on rocky ground. The narrator reveals that Tamuera, as a container ship crew member, could earn up to 700 US dollars monthly, a sum that would significantly improve his financial standing.
